class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Una placa dedicada al 30 aniversario de la muerte de César Chávez, en honor a él y a la “hermana Dolores Huerta”, forma parte del monumento de Haymarket en el West Loop.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>El miércoles, varios funcionarios latinos de Chicago expresaron ese sentimiento y emitieron declaraciones de apoyo a las mujeres.</p><p>"Me solidarizo incondicionalmente con todas las sobrevivientes del abuso de César Chávez y estoy comprometida a construir un mundo donde la violencia sexual, el acoso sexual y la explotación sexual sean erradicados, y quienes perpetúan estas injusticias sean responsabilizados”, dijo la representante estadounidense Delia Ramírez.</p><p>El representante estadounidense Jesús “Chuy” García dijo que las sobrevivientes que han salido a hablar sobre Chávez han mostrado valentía al contar sus historias. “Es difícil y doloroso ver a alguien que a quien respetabas y en quien confiabas como líder sea expuesto de esta manera. Se siente como una traición, y los abusadores, en cualquier nivel, deberían ser responsabilizados”.</p><p>El concejal Andre Vázquez (40º) dijo que no hay un legado que valga la pena preservar si depende de sacrificar la humanidad de otra persona.</p><p>“César Chávez es un hombre. Cualquier esfuerzo por reducir los movimientos de los trabajadores agrícolas —y mucho menos el movimiento por los derechos de los trabajadores latinos en su conjunto— a un solo hombre perjudica a todas las personas que han luchado por proteger los derechos y la dignidad de sus compañeros trabajadores: personas como Dolores Huerta, Ana Murguía y Debra Rojas, entre muchas otras”, aseguró Vázquez.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d5c83e" name="image-d5c83e"></a> <picture class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Anthony Martínez, un activista comunitario en el lado sureste de Chicago, a la derecha, junto a Dolores Huerta.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Huerta dijo que no sabía que Chávez había lastimado a otras mujeres y condenó sus acciones, pero enfatizó que el movimiento de los trabajadores agrícolas es más grande que una sola persona.</p><p>“Las acciones de César no disminuyen las mejoras permanentes logradas para los trabajadores agrícolas con la ayuda de miles de personas”, dijo. “Debemos seguir involucrándonos y apoyando a nuestra comunidad, que necesita abogacía y activismo ahora más que nunca”.</p><p>El activista comunitario del lado suroreste, Anthony Martínez, miembro del grupo conocido como Brown Berets, similar al grupo de los Panteras Negras, dijo que conoció a Huerta en varias ocasiones en mítines políticos. Martinez, de 46 años, apoya que Huerta hable su verdad.</p><p>“Ella fue realmente quien hizo más, pero en aquel entonces el hombre tenía que liderar para poder ser escuchado. Dejaron que César Chávez tomara la delantera”, dijo Martínez. “Realmente no quiero culparla por hablar tarde sobre esto”.</p><p>Anderson Guerrero dijo que el enfoque debe permanecer en las sobrevivientes, no en Chávez.</p><p>“Necesitamos... permitirles tiempo para procesar y reflexionar sobre lo que esto significa para ellas, lo que significa para sus recuerdos de ser parte de un movimiento, y darles el espacio para ser creativas y decidir cómo quieren que sea el futuro”, dijo. “Ya sea apoyando a Dolores Huerta y su legado, bajando murales, o quizás creando otra oportunidades que aún no están en nuestra imaginación”.</p><p><i>Con información de la Associated Press </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Michael Puente

entries.title: Chicago Plan Commission gives first approvals to 340-unit apartment tower in Lincoln Park
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Plan Commission gave the first approval Thursday to a high-rise in an area of Lincoln Park that’s gradually becoming more dense.</p><p>Honore Properties and Peerless Development's $102 million project at 1415 N. Dayton St. would be 28 stories and bring 340 apartments to the southern edge of Lincoln Park, across from Goose Island and the Salt Shed.</p><p>It’s an area where taller buildings are being pitched, Angela Spadoni, principal at bKL Architecture, said. The City Council has already approved a 37-story residential tower nearby at 1565 N. Clybourn Ave.<b> </b>There’s also a 27-story condo building planned at 860 W. Blackhawk St.</p><p>Spadoni, whose firm designed the Dayton Street project, said the intersection of Halsted Street and North Avenue is “very vibrant,” and the abundant transportation options nearby makes it an ideal site for apartments.</p><p>If the proposal is approved by the City Council, the developers will demolish the site's existing four buildings — 1415 N. Dayton St., 821 W. Eastman St., 811 W. Evergreen Ave. and 1415 N. Kingsbury St. — and make additional improvements to the surrounding street. </p><p>The sidewalks along Dayton and Evergreen streets will be widened to 6 feet, and streetscaping will be added. Dayton Street’s sidewalk is currently about 4 feet wide, making it too narrow for pedestrians.</p><p>The building would include studios up to three-bedroom apartments, with 68 affordable units. The affordable units would be for those making an average 60% of the area median income. In Chicago, that's $50,400 annually for an individual renter. </p><p>Two amenity decks — with the rooftop deck including a pool — are planned.</p><p>Commissioners also heard a presentation from Advocate Health Care for its <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2024/12/17/advocate-health-care-replace-trinity-hospital" >new hospital</a> at the old U.S. Steel South Works site. The 52-bed hospital will open as Advocate Trinity Hospital. The five-story hospital will include 36 surgery beds, four intensive car unit beds, eight observation beds, a four-bed dialysis unit and an emergency room with 16 bays. Once open, the hospital system will close its 205-bed hospital, Trinity, at 2320 E. 93rd St., which has been operating since 1895. That property will be demolished become green space.</p><p>The new hospital is part of Advocate’s $1 billion investment to close Chicago’s 30-year life expectancy gap between residents on the South and North sides.</p><p>Based on community feedback, Advocate Trinity Hospital President Michelle Blakely said it added community gardens to its plans, along with respite spaces for families.</p><p>“We're creating an absolute community, as opposed to simply a hospital,” Blakely told commissioners. “We're bringing the community into the space by including respite spaces and walking trails and other things that become attractive to the way that the community engages with the hospital and helps us further our intention to create health and wellness.”</p><p>The commission also approved plans for a 66-unit residential building in the north end of Fulton Market.</p><p>The building, at 1201 W. Kinzie St., is “a straightforward and small-ish project for this part of the Fulton Market,” Scott Borstein, attorney at Neal & Leroy on behalf of the developer, said. </p><p>Dirk Denison Architects Managing Director Justin DeGroff said the firm designed the building to look like three smaller buildings. The buildings feature green brick and Juliet balconies — a unique design for the area.</p><p>Ald. Walter “Red” Burnett (27th) praised the project, which he said bridges the two sides of Hubbard Street, where the south portion is the typical Fulton Market high-rises and north of Hubbard Street has more townhomes and residential development.</p><p>“This is a nice in-between,” Burnett said.</p>
entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: Former Aspira students try to adapt after troubled charter operator closes schools midyear
entries.summary: <p>Efrain Aguirre got lost as he tried to find his classes in Schurz High School’s massive brown-brick building that has four floors and takes up an entire city block.</p><p>But he felt encouraged when Schurz students graciously pointed him in the right direction. That kindness gave him “a little confidence” that he would eventually feel okay about going to school there.</p><p>It has only been a week since the junior left Aspira Business & Finance High School, one of two schools run by the financially distressed Aspira charter network that is in the process of an unprecedented midyear school closing.</p><p>At a welcoming event in Schurz’s gym on Wednesday, Efrain said he still felt “questionable” and “worried” about transferring to the neighborhood high school in Irving Park on the city’s Northwest Side.</p><p>“I am pretty new here and I just started talking to people to see if I could find friends,” said the skinny teenager wearing a blue sweatshirt, who was the only one from his friend group to enroll in Schurz.</p><div class="Enhancement" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d52bc11/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+424/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff8%2F9c%2F28361df84356bdab740422c1e131%2Faspirarevoke-260322-20.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d52bc11/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+424/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff8%2F9c%2F28361df84356bdab740422c1e131%2Faspirarevoke-260322-20.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Transfer students often find Schurz High School’s large brick building difficult to navigate. The school’s principal noted that as she asked her students to welcome former Aspira students to their campus.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As Aspira closes its schools, Chicago Public Schools faces some important questions. Among them: Can it successfully usher some 570 teenagers to new schools in March without some students falling through the cracks? And can it do anything now to make sure students at other privately run schools with financial issues don’t experience a similar kind of disruption?</p><p>CPS officials have promised to provide bus cards, tutoring and mental health support to Aspira students and to waive their student fees. The district is also putting a transition coordinator at schools that take in a lot of students, like Schurz.</p><p>Conrad Timbers-Ausar, the interim chief of the CPS department that oversees charters, said district staff are reaching out to each Aspira student to make sure they get into a school “that meets their individual needs.” He said some students are still “maybe not sure where they want to go.”</p><p>Over the past two weeks, more than 540 students left Aspira schools. About 30 students were still officially enrolled as of Thursday at Aspira’s two campuses.</p><p>Several publicly funded, privately run schools in Chicago have closed in recent years. But Aspira’s wind-down is different from these other schools, almost all of which closed in the summer, allowing students to transfer at the start of a new school year, when other new students are showing up, too.</p><p>Another difference: Aspira leaders initially fought their closure and sent mixed messages to families about whether they should find new schools, making the transition even harder for some students.</p><h3><b>Charter trouble gives neighborhood high school a boost</b></h3><p>Aspira’s financial troubles have been apparent for months. CEO Edgar Lopez announced in November that the charter network did not have enough money to make it through the school year. The district provided cash advances this fall, but last month CPS officials said the amount Aspira needed was beyond what they could legally provide.</p><p>Before Aspira students were officially told to transfer, some said they were nervous to attend a nearby high school, like Schurz, because they’d heard about safety issues.</p><p>Lopez says some students have already come back to Aspira, complaining about the neighborhood schools they transferred into. He said he was concerned that CPS encouraged students to attend four “underperforming neighborhood schools.” Now he’s urging his former students to attend Intrinsic Schools’ Belmont campus, a nearby charter that’s one of the higher performing schools in the area.</p><p>But for Schurz and other neighborhood high schools, the closing of Aspira’s campuses represents an opportunity. They have seen enrollment declines for two decades as families have had fewer children, moved out of the city, or chosen to send their children to other schools. Taking in lots of students midyear could end up stabilizing their enrollment.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b70000" name="image-b70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/86da86c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F41%2Fa3%2Fe6d9282b48bab0dc4a638941fa30%2Faspirarevoke-260322-04.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ef9073c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F41%2Fa3%2Fe6d9282b48bab0dc4a638941fa30%2Faspirarevoke-260322-04.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Schurz Principal Heidy Moran knew it was important to take extra steps to support students transferring in from Aspira. She called an all-school virtual meeting and hosted a welcoming fair to help with the transition.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Twenty-five years ago, Schurz’s hulking building was almost at capacity with more than 2,500 students. But it started this school year with about 1,000.</p><p>Schurz’s principal, Heidy Moran, knew it was important to take extra steps to welcome her new students.</p><p>Once it became clear that Aspira’s schools were shutting down, Moran had the entire student body log into the Google Meet app for a virtual town hall. </p><p>“She gave this talk to everybody, saying, ‘Listen, Schurz is a place where we welcomed you,” recounted Praise Lee, who coordinates the school’s International Baccalaureate program. “‘Think back to the first time that you were at our school, either as a freshman or as a transfer student, and what did that feel like for you? What we want to do is open our hearts and our doors to people who need a place to study.’”</p><p>Lee says that message set the tone as more than 200 Aspira students enrolled at Schurz in the three weeks that followed.</p><p>“We are a little bit shell shocked by it, but also it energized us,” Lee said.</p><p>Moran previously taught at a charter elementary school on the Far South Side, a job she loved.</p><p>But she says being at a neighborhood school has “totally transformed her thinking.” She now sees that district-run schools are chock-full of opportunities and that negative reputations can stick to neighborhood schools after they are no longer true.</p><h3><b>How Schurz students are welcoming classmates from Aspira</b></h3><p>Before the Aspira students enrolled, Moran interviewed other students who previously transferred into Schurz. She said they told her that they were “overwhelmed by the size of the building” and, while the teachers were welcoming, the students were “friendly, but not all that open.” That informed what she said at the virtual meeting.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-990000" name="image-990000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A welcome sign greeting new students hung on the lockers at Schurz High School on Wednesday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Moran and her staff have done other things to welcome the Aspira students, too. Current Schurz students were paired with Aspira students to act as mentors. In the makerspace on the second floor, new Schurz stickers are being printed to affix to laptops. And the digital media arts teacher is having students create a book of pictures featuring the Aspira teens, who won’t make the Schurz yearbook that already went to the printer.</p><p>Moran put together the welcoming event earlier this week, though the energetic young principal noted it was “kind of wild” to hold an event in March like the one that typically happens at the beginning of the school year.</p><p>After hearing from Moran and other school administrators, Aspira students and their parents browsed an activity fair with tables manned by current students, who discussed Schurz’s sports and academic offerings.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ba0000" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Schurz High School junior Arianna Olvera made sure students who transferred in from Aspira knew about the school’s dual language program that offers classes in English and Spanish.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Arianna Olvera, a junior, talked with students about the school’s dual language program, which allows students to take classes in both English and Spanish.</p><p>Speaking in Spanish, Arianna told the Aspira students that they can find a home at Schurz, which like Aspira, has a largely Latino student body with many teens who are learning English.</p><p>She said the dual language program helps students learn and retain their native tongue and have pride in their culture.</p><p>“The Spanish-speaking teachers are very good to us,” she said. “They help us progress, they help us feel comfortable at school and feel good about ourselves. This program is based on us immigrant students to feel comfortable, to feel at peace.”</p><p>At the table about the International Baccalaureate program, which offers advanced coursework that can translate to college credit, three young women told prospective students that the project-based classes are hard, but they can pay off.</p><p>“It is very rigorous,” junior Andrea Galvan said.</p><p>Coming in freshman year, they said they had no idea what IB was or that Schurz offered it. They said it is just one way that the high school is different from the way it is perceived from the outside.</p><p>They also bristle at the idea that Schurz is not safe.</p><p>“I hate that,” Andrea said. “Any school has people who are obviously not doing well, people who are in bad groups and honestly, I feel like if they just stay clear of those types of people then they'll be fine.”</p><p>As Aspira parents and students tried to keep an open mind about Schurz, many still felt unsettled by the sudden closures.</p><p>Efrain missed his World Language teacher at Aspira, who was not only an engaging instructor, but also allowed him and his friends to come to his room during lunch to play video games.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Senior Jonathan Escobar said he misses his teachers at Aspira and that transferring to Schurz, even with classmates from Aspira, feels like “coming to a new home.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Looking around the gym at the welcome fair tables, Efrain said he is not really a “club-joining type of person,” but he would like to find a space at Schurz like the one he had at Aspira.</p><p>Senior Jonathan Escobar said some of his friends are also at Schurz, but it doesn’t feel the same walking the halls.</p><p>“My teachers, my classes, I miss everything,” Jonathan said. “It is sad to leave the old school I used to be at. It feels like coming to a new home.”</p><p>Mom Griselda Hermosillo said she and her daughter, Estefani Martin, a junior, feels welcomed at Schurz so far, and she’s hopeful that it will be a good place, emotionally, for her daughter. Estefani said it’s been a relief to see many of her Aspira friends in the hallways at Schurz.</p><p>But Hermosillo feels like someone should be held responsible for the abrupt closing of Aspira’s schools. She doesn’t understand why parents weren’t informed about the troubles sooner.</p><p>“Nobody is talking about it anymore and it feels like this period has passed and we are going to move on,” she said.</p>
entries.author: Sarah Karp | WBEZ

entries.title: MLS will play a shortened season in early 2027 before moving to new schedule
entries.summary: <p>Major League Soccer will play a shortened 14-game regular season in early 2027 before the league switches to a schedule that better aligns with those of its international counterparts.</p><p>The league said Thursday that the so-called "sprint season" will run from February to May 2027, with each of the league's 30 teams playing 14 games — seven at home and seven on the road — solely against conference opponents.</p><p>The top eight teams from each conference will advance to the single-elimination MLS Cup playoffs, culminating with a championship game between the Eastern and Western Conference champions. Five teams will qualify for the 2028 CONCACAF Champions Cup and 18 teams, nine from each conference, will qualify for the 2028 Leagues Cup based on performance during the sprint season.</p><p>The league will then turn to a 2027-28 season that kicks off in July 2027. The playoffs and league championship will be played in May 2028.</p><p>The move to a summer-to-spring calendar aims to put MLS in a more competitive position for player transfers, while also freeing up its players for national team duties during the summer, when many major international tournaments take place. The current season began in February and will wrap up with the MLS Cup final in December.</p><p>MLS owners voted last year to make the switch.</p><p>The new MLS schedule includes an extended break during the winter, with just a few games played in early December and no games in January before resuming in early to mid-February.</p><p>"This is an opportunity for us to eliminate the competition that we've had for our playoffs as they exist today in a very crowded time of the year," MLS Commissioner Don Garber said when the new season format was approved. "It allows us to be aligned with the international transfer windows, which we think is incredibly important. It gives us a wide variety of opportunities that will expand our ability to be on this path to be one of the top and leading leagues in the world."</p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: New owner of The Hideout plans to continue venue’s legacy as a cultural institution and community hub
entries.summary: <p>For the first time in 30 years, The Hideout has a new owner.</p><p>Teri O’Brien — a performing artist, independent festival producer and one-time employee of the West Town concert venue — is taking the reins from the husband-and-wife team of Tim and Katie Tuten and brothers Mike and Jim Hinchsliff, the venue <a class="Link" href="https://www.hideoutchicago.com/announcement" target="_blank" ><u>announced</u></a> Thursday, according to a post on its website.</p><p>The reasons behind the transition and the terms of the agreement were not disclosed, but O’Brien (who splits her time between Nashville and Chicago) said that conversations with the former owners started in February 2025. “I always dreamed of owning a music venue and I told [the Tutens] that in passing,” she told the Sun-Times. “They approached me last year … and this just felt like the right moment in my life.”</p><p>The Tutens and Hinchsliffs first purchased the converted century-old house along the North Branch of the Chicago River in 1996. Over the years, they’ve turned it into a cultural institution and community hub that was recognized as a “best” music venue by publications like Rolling Stone and USA Today and was a pit stop for figures like Anthony Bourdain.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatLeft> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7f0000" name="image-7f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Teri O’Brien — a performing artist, independent festival producer and one-time employee of the West Town concert venue — is the new owner of The Hideout.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Lindsey Torres</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Hideout has hosted homegrown and national music acts including Andrew Bird, Wilco, Mavis Staples and the storied Hideout Block Party; comedy nights with Hannibal Buress and Ramy Youssef; political offerings including events with Politico and the ongoing panel event “Show Up Chicago”; as well as community events like the regularly scheduled Soup & Bread nights that aim to fight hunger in Chicago.</p><p>“The Hideout will stay the Hideout,” Tim Tuten said in a statement. “It will remain independent, creative and rooted in the community that built it. Teri knows our room, the people and our history, and she has the heart to carry it forward.”</p><p>“The Hideout works because of the people who gather in it. That doesn’t change with this transition, in fact it will grow,” Katie Tuten added in another statement. The Tutens were not available for additional comment.</p><p>Bird noted how important the venue has been to the local music community.</p><p>”The Hideout is one of only a few venues that exists because of and is a manifestation of the personalities of its owners, Tim and Katie Tuten,” the singer-songwriter who hails from Lake Bluff said in a statement. “The fact that it’s being passed to an employee is definitely encouraging that it will continue in its original spirit and not be acquiring flat screens behind the bar.”</p><p>“I feel like I’ve seen all sides of the music industry,” said O’Brien of the knowledge she brings as The Hideout’s new owner. She’s also a recording and touring artist performing under the stage name Brontë Fall. “I’ve spent the last decade touring and being a performing artist, booking my own shows. I feel like I know the ins and outs of what it takes to run the venue because I worked there. I know what it’s like to be a performer, so this was the perfect storm for me.”</p><p>Before assuming ownership of The Hideout, O’Brien worked at the venue from 2015-2018 in an artist hospitality position. Prior to that, she was part of the backstage operations team at Ravinia. O’Brien also acts as a producer of the annual Chicago indie music event Big Heart Fest, held at Chief O’Neill’s in Avondale every June; it was created as a tribute to her late father, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/obituaries/2022/7/12/23205692/john-obrien-futures-trader-family-owned-brokerage" target="_blank" ><u>John W. O’Brien</u></a> who ran the financial brokerage firm R.J. O’Brien & Associates and “loved music and community,” said O’Brien.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-5d0000" name="image-5d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/52eae63/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F93%2F15%2F0c67e31f4fed9bd72c2216d9e369%2Fwatchdogs-081521-13.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7fb4c54/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F93%2F15%2F0c67e31f4fed9bd72c2216d9e369%2Fwatchdogs-081521-13.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/52eae63/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F93%2F15%2F0c67e31f4fed9bd72c2216d9e369%2Fwatchdogs-081521-13.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“The Hideout works because of the people who gather in it. That doesn’t change with this transition, in fact it will grow,” said former Hideout owner Katie Tuten of the change of ownership at the venue she’s co-owned since 1996.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The ownership switchup at The Hideout comes at a critical juncture for Chicago’s independent music venues; as the Sun-Times <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/02/03/chicago-indie-venues-health-metro-ramova-salt-shed" target="_blank" ><u>previously reported</u></a>, citing data from the Chicago Independent Venue League (CIVL), only one in four are currently profitable. The Hideout, notably, is also a founding member of CIVL, which grew out of the previous owners’ fight against Live Nation’s failed Lincoln Yards project.</p><p>“Fortunately The Hideout is stable,” O’Brien said. “After touring for nearly a decade all over the country I see it, though. I see that venues are hurting and asking for money to stay open. I know the industry is in flux.”</p><p>The new owner said she’s looking into building partnerships and alliances with the city and nonprofit organizations as well as working with The Hideout’s current 26-member staff and the community to gather ideas for sustainability of the venue. Some of them include adding nonalcoholic drinks and mocktails to the bar to cater to changing preferences with patrons and possibly adding podcasting to the mix of entertainment booked at the club.</p><p>But much will stay the same as O’Brien looks to maintain the programming and offerings that make The Hideout unique.</p><p>“It’s so rare to see a music club that’s not just a music club. I want to continue with that legacy and build on the community and the vibe it’s always had,” she said. “I think there’s so many fun things we can do to continue bringing it into the 21st century.”</p>
entries.author: Selena Fragassi

entries.title: 'Broadview Six' ICE protesters can see White House communications about their case — if they exist
entries.summary: <p>If the White House encouraged<b> </b>Chicago’s U.S. attorney's office in writing to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/29/kat-abughazaleh-conspiracy-indictment-broadview-protests-donald-trump-deportation-campaign" target="_blank" >bring a conspiracy<b> </b>indictment</a> against six<b> </b>immigration protesters last fall, defense attorneys should be allowed to see it, a prosecutor agreed Thursday.</p><p>But Assistant U.S. Attorney William<b> </b>Hogan<b> </b>said he didn’t expect to find anything related to the case against the so-called “Broadview Six.” Hogan made the comment while speaking to U.S. District Judge<b> </b>April Perry about recent defense motions in the case.</p><p>“To my knowledge — I’m not saying that I know for sure — but my understanding is there is no such communication,” Hogan said. “Certainly, [there] was none before the indictment.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/16/chicago-broadview-six-kat-abughazaleh-midway-blitz-trump-ice" >Remaining ‘Broadview Six’ defendants want conspiracy charge tossed, argue protesting isn’t a crime</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Hogan also confirmed for the judge that prosecutors still expect to take four of the original six defendants to trial. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-midway-blitz-immigration-trump-kat-abughazaleh-sharp-joselyn-walsh-broadview-six" target="_blank" >The feds last week dropped charges</a> against musician<b> </b>Joselyn<b> </b>Walsh<b> </b>and former Cook County Board candidate Catherine “Cat” Sharp.</p><p>Still charged are<b> </b>former congressional candidate Kat<b> </b>Abughazaleh, Oak Park village trustee Brian Straw, 45th Ward Democratic committeeperson Michael<b> </b>Rabbitt and Andre Martin,<b> </b>a member of Abughazaleh’s campaign staff. They’re all set for trial May 26.</p><p>Straw was the only defendant to attend Thursday’s hearing.<b> </b>Each of the four remaining defendants are involved in local Democratic politics.<b> </b><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/17/us-house-illinois-9th-congressional-district-primary-results" target="_blank" >Abughazaleh placed second</a> in Tuesday’s primary for the Democratic nomination for Illinois’ 9th Congressional District seat, won by Evanston Mayor Daniel Biss.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-4c0000" name="image-4c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/844c743/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2Ff5%2Fabe4ba2b44f1b2989a0f9c14d81f%2Fbviewsix-260320-05.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7ac024c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2Ff5%2Fabe4ba2b44f1b2989a0f9c14d81f%2Fbviewsix-260320-05.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d36bfd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2Ff5%2Fabe4ba2b44f1b2989a0f9c14d81f%2Fbviewsix-260320-05.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Oak Park Village Trustee Brian Straw leaves the Dirksen Federal Courthouse after a hearing in the so-called "Broadview Six" case on Thursday, March 19, 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d36bfd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2Ff5%2Fabe4ba2b44f1b2989a0f9c14d81f%2Fbviewsix-260320-05.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/50f25ad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2Ff5%2Fabe4ba2b44f1b2989a0f9c14d81f%2Fbviewsix-260320-05.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d36bfd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2Ff5%2Fabe4ba2b44f1b2989a0f9c14d81f%2Fbviewsix-260320-05.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Oak Park Village Trustee Brian Straw leaves the Dirksen Federal Courthouse after a hearing in the so-called “Broadview Six” case on Thursday, March 19, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The four are accused of a conspiracy to impede a federal agent. The charges revolve around events on the morning of Sept. 26 outside the<b> </b>U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility in Broadview.</p><p>An indictment alleges that, while a federal agent drove a vehicle toward the ICE facility, the defendants and others surrounded it. Members of the larger crowd allegedly banged on the vehicle, pushed against it, scratched it and even etched the word “PIG” into it.</p><p>The crowd allegedly broke a side mirror and a rear windshield wiper and forced the agent “to drive at an extremely slow rate of speed.”</p><p>However, video also shows that, after the agent drove the vehicle into the crowd, Abughazaleh grabbed a bullhorn and told fellow protesters, “that’s private property back there, come back here now.”</p><p>Defense attorneys say the agent did not wait for police to clear a path, did not identify himself and did not order the crowd to move aside.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-midway-blitz-immigration-trump-kat-abughazaleh-sharp-joselyn-walsh-broadview-six" >Broadview 4? Feds drop conspiracy charges against 2 ICE protesters, including Dem who ended campaign</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Perry on Thursday asked Hogan<b> </b>to confirm that U.S. Attorney Andrew Boutros’ office received no communication from the White House encouraging the prosecution. Defense attorneys sought that and other records in a motion filed late last week. Perry spent much of Thursday’s hearing walking both sides through each defense request.</p><p>Along the way, Hogan told Perry<b> </b>the person who scratched the word “PIG”<b> </b>into the agent’s vehicle “was not identified.”<b> </b>The prosecutor also agreed that, if any internal communication exists among the feds suggesting people should be prosecuted for their political beliefs, that should likely be turned over to the defense.</p><p>Finally, the lawyers discussed a <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/19/us/politics/justice-dept-prosecute-protesters.html?unlocked_article_code=1.UVA.M2DW.BUUcCgAY4KzX&smid=url-share" target="_blank" >New York Times article</a> published Thursday<b> </b>about criminal charges brought nationally against protesters as a result of President Donald Trump’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2025/09/countering-domestic-terrorism-and-organized-political-violence/" target="_blank" >National Security Presidential Memorandum 7</a>. Known as NSPM-7, it addresses “domestic terrorism and organized political violence” and cites the assassination of Charlie Kirk.</p><p>Trump issued the memo<b> </b>Sept. 25, one day before the incident at Broadview that led to the conspiracy indictment against the Chicago protesters.</p><p>Hogan told the judge he was unaware of the memo until the New York Times article was emailed to him by defense attorneys<b> </b>about two hours before Thursday’s hearing.</p><p>“We’ll look into that,” he said.</p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: MLB viewing guide for Cubs, White Sox fans
entries.summary: <p>You know what I can’t stand? The complete answer is as long as the list of FAST channels you never watch on your connected TV. But for the purpose of this column, I’ll give you one: internet stories about an upcoming game with a headline that begins “How to watch …”</p><p>Those stories are intended to be helpful. I find them insulting. You know how I watch a game? I turn on the TV, enter the channel number and sit down. Call me old-fashioned, but that really should be all it takes.</p><p>The issue isn’t HOW to watch (with my eyes, duh!). It’s WHERE to watch. Sports properties seem to be moving all over the map, and you have to follow along closely to keep up. This is what I do. You see that calendar on the right? I fill that in. I’ve entered channels you’ve never watched.</p><p>Which brings us to the MLB season that starts with one game on Wednesday. Why? Because of TV — streaming, actually.</p><p>That’s just one aspect of MLB’s new three-year media-rights deals. I won’t explain HOW to watch the games. You’re smart enough to figure that out. But here’s a guide for WHERE to find them and how many will include the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a>.</p><h3>Netflix</h3><p>Welcome to the party, pal! The world’s largest paid streaming service will air an MLB game in the United States for the first time when the Yankees visit the Giants at 7:05 p.m. Wednesday. Netflix will carry the stand-alone season opener in 2027 and ’28, as well (barring a labor stoppage, of course).</p><p>Netflix also will air the Home Run Derby on July 13 in Philadelphia and the “Field of Dreams” game Aug. 13 between the Phillies and Twins. It could be just the beginning of a relationship between MLB and Netflix, which delivered the entire World Baseball Classic to Japan. Could that happen here?</p><p><b>Cubs/Sox games:</b> None.</p><h3>NBC/NBCSN/Peacock</h3><p>The second game of the MLB season (Pirates at Mets, 12:15 p.m. Thursday) will air on NBC, which is back with baseball again. NBC also will show the World Series champion Dodgers host the Diamondbacks at 7:30.</p><p>But the network’s biggest day during the regular season will be Sundays when Peacock streams the “Sunday Leadoff” package it originally carried in 2022-23 and NBC airs the “Sunday Night Baseball” franchise that belonged to ESPN. Former White Sox TV voice <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/03/04/nbc-sports-former-white-sox-tv-voice-jason-benetti-sunday-night-baseball-brooks-boyer-tigers-fox" >Jason Benetti will be NBC’s lead voice</a> for “SNF.” He’ll be joined by analysts from the participating teams.</p><p>Here’s where it gets tricky: Most games exclusive to Peacock, such as “Sunday Leadoff” games, will simulcast on NBC Sports Network. Some Sunday night games will air on Peacock and simulcast on NBCSN because of conflicts on NBC with previously negotiated contracts.</p><p>Also, NBC will air the Futures Game on All-Star Sunday, Peacock will carry every game July 5 as part of “Star-Spangled Sunday” and NBC, NBCSN and Peacock will air every game of the wild-card round.</p><p><b>Cubs/Sox games:</b> The Cubs are scheduled for three appearances on “Sunday Night Baseball” (May 31 at Cardinals, June 7 vs. Giants, Aug. 30 vs. Reds at 2 p.m.), and the Sox have one on “Sunday Leadoff” (July 19 at Blue Jays, 11 a.m.). Both will be on Peacock on July 5.</p><h3>ESPN</h3><p>After 36 seasons, ESPN is out of the “Sunday Night Baseball” business, but it’s still in the MLB business. The network’s new exclusive schedule will focus on the summer, with 23 of its 30 games airing in June, July and August. ESPN also will air games on Jackie Robinson Day (April 15) and Memorial Day (May 25), as well as the Little League Classic between the Braves and Brewers (Aug. 23). And baseball returns to ABC with a three-game schedule.</p><p><b>Cubs/Sox games:</b> The Cubs will appear on two ABC broadcasts (June 14 at Giants and Aug. 16 vs. Cardinals, both Sundays at 2 p.m.). ESPN’s schedule is still mostly to be determined.</p><h3>Fox/FS1</h3><p>Fox Sports will air more than 85 games, including 23 on Saturdays on “Baseball Night in America.” Fox also has the All-Star Game and World Series, and Fox and FS1 will have games in the NL championship and division series.</p><p><b>Cubs/Sox games:</b> The Cubs are scheduled for six appearances on Fox, the first coming April 4 at the Guardians, and two on FS1 that are non-exclusive.</p><h3>TBS</h3><p>The network will air games on Tuesday nights in the regular season, as well as in the AL championship and division series.</p><p><b>Cubs/Sox games:</b> TBS released its first-half schedule, which has the Cubs making two appearances. Those broadcasts will be blacked out in Chicago because Marquee Sports Network will carry the games.</p><h3>Apple TV</h3><p>The streamer will air two exclusive games on Friday nights in the regular season.</p><p><b>Cubs/Sox games:</b> The Cubs have three appearances in the first half (April 24 at Dodgers, May 22 vs. Astros at 1:20 p.m. and June 26 at Brewers).</p><h3>MLB Network</h3><p>The 24/7 home for baseball will air games almost every day, but Cubs and Sox games will be blacked out locally because they’ll air on Marquee and Chicago Sports Network, respectively.</p><h3>Chicago Sports Network</h3><p>CHSN will carry almost every Sox game. It also will simulcast 10 over the air on The U (WCIU-Channel 26), including four games against the Cubs.</p><h3>Marquee Sports Network</h3><p>Marquee will carry a vast majority of Cubs games, but it already has lost 14 to national networks. That number will increase.<br></p>
entries.author: Jeff Agrest

entries.title: Transit security, improved service should be top priorities of incoming NITA board: survey
entries.summary: <p>Chicago-area commuters want the incoming Northern Illinois Transit Authority to focus on improving security and bus and rail service, according to a new survey by the Regional Transportation Authority.</p><p>More than 1,400 riders surveyed in December and January listed "public safety" and "improving service" to be top priorities of the NITA board, which replaces the RTA on June 1.</p><p>Those results took priority over "expanding service" and "seamless and affordable fares."</p><p>The survey results were shared at the RTA's Thursday board meeting. Some of the meeting was focused on transition planning to the NITA board this summer. That board is mandated by the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2025/12/16/illinois-transit-bill-signed-pritzker" target="_blank" >major public transit law signed by Gov. JB Pritzker in December</a> that will overhaul the CTA, Metra and Pace, and infuse them with more than $1 billion in new yearly funding.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div and improving service.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>RTA board meeting screengrab</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The survey results, <a class="Link" href="https://experience.arcgis.com/experience/b1a29e850bf44bf087e01277eabfcaad/page/Surveys" target="_blank" >posted online</a>, also found that over two-thirds of riders said their feeling of personal safety "remained about the same" over the previous three months. About a quarter said their feeling of safety decreased over that time, while about 8% said it improved.</p><p>The survey also found that satisfaction with "cleanliness" and "personal security" were rated the lowest of all other categories, rated an average of 5.6 and 5.7 respectively on a 1-10 scale.</p><p>The survey results were shared as the federal government ramps up pressure on the Chicago Transit Authority to improve security of riders and employees following a series of high-profile assaults. The Sun-Times reported last month that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/02/chicago-cta-transit-crime-assault-battery-leerhsen" target="_blank" >aggravated assaults and batteries have reached historic highs</a> on the CTA.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9f1546c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2104x1181+0+26/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0b%2Ff3%2F4fc06c94438c90633ec5b7736c85%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-19-at-10-01-22-am.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A public safety survey presented at the Regional Transportation Authority’s March 19, 2026, board meeting.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Livestream screengrab</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The NITA law calls on the Cook County sheriff's office to study the possibility of creating a transit-wide police force. Currently, only Metra has its own police force. The CTA is currently patrolled by Chicago police and other local agencies, as is Pace.</p><h3>FTA walks back funding threat on CTA, ramps up pressure on state</h3><p>The Federal Transit Administration this week tempered its threat to cut $50 million in funding to the CTA, but increased pressure on the Illinois Department of Transportation. It follows the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/03/10/chicago-transit-authority-cta-crime-security-plan-fta" target="_blank" >CTA issuing its second revised safety plan</a> to the FTA last week that boosts the police presence on the system by 75%, with the addition of sheriff's deputies who are patrolling trains.</p><p>In a <a class="Link" href="https://www.transit.dot.gov/sites/fta.dot.gov/files/2026-03/FTA-Letter-to-CTA-on-Revised-Security-Enhancement-Plan.pdf" target="_blank" >letter</a> sent to the CTA on Tuesday, the FTA said it is "not withholding federal funding at this time," but reserves the right to cut funding "without further notice" if the CTA does not follow through with its commitment to the FTA's initial demands issued in December. The FTA promptly rejected the CTA's first response to the FTA's demand in December, which boosted K9 units and CPD's staffing.</p><p>The FTA also issued a new demand this week to IDOT, which the <a class="Link" href="https://www.transportation.gov/briefing-room/trumps-transportation-secretary-sean-p-duffy-cracks-down-illinois-dot-mismanagement" target="_blank" >FTA claimed</a> has failed in its oversight role over the CTA. The FTA claims that IDOT has not used all of its federally issued funds meant to be used in that oversight role.</p><p>"IDOT has not exercised its authority, resources, or responsibilities at a level commensurate with CTA’s size, complexity, or safety risk profile," the FTA wrote in its <a class="Link" href="https://www.transit.dot.gov/sites/fta.dot.gov/files/2026-03/FTA-Special-Directive-26-1.pdf" target="_blank" >directive</a> to IDOT.</p><h3>Metra to pilot fare collection on platforms</h3><p>Some Metra commuters may have to show their Ventra ticket or paper ticket on downtown platforms, instead of to a conductor on a train, in a pilot program set to begin April 6. </p><p>Metra's board on Thursday approved a plan to test handheld ticket scanners that could help the incoming NITA board transition to integrated tickets that can be used across the CTA, Metra and Pace.</p><p>The test will initially be conducted on off-peak trains to avoid jams, the agency said in a statement.</p>
entries.author: David Struett

entries.title: Mayor Johnson fires deputy mayor of public safety amid allegations of toxic work environment, self-promotion
entries.summary: <p>The quarterback of the community safety plan that Mayor Brandon Johnson credits for historic drops in violent crime was abruptly fired Thursday amid allegations of a hostile work environment and self-promotion.</p><p>Deputy Mayor of Community Safety<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2023/5/19/23730163/brandon-johnson-deputy-mayor-community-safety-garien-gatewood" > Garien Gatewood</a> was called into a meeting with Johnson’s chief of staff Cristina Pacione-Zayas and top aide Jason Lee and told they were “moving in a different direction.”</p><p>“They told me they wanted to work more on policy,” Gatewood told the Sun-Times.</p><p>When asked what that meant, Gatewood responded: “That’s the question you have to ask them.”</p><p>"People shouldn’t be allowed to say they don’t talk about personnel matters," he added. "If people believe it’s working and we are clear who was helping this work together… then all I can do is hope the work continues and people's lives continue to be saved."</p><p>One of Gatewood's top deputies, Manuel Whitfield, director of violence prevention and community safety, was also fired.</p><p>The mayor’s office put out a statement confirming the change but saying very little else. First Deputy Jason Sanford will now serve in the interim role.</p><p>The office maintained their longstanding policy of not discussing personnel issues.</p><p>However, sources close to the mayor’s senior staff said the environment on Gatewood’s team was "deteriorating rapidly,” and that multiple team members made reports of “hostile gestures” toward women “not aggressively but in a way that made them uncomfortable.”</p><p>“There were real concerns about this type of behavior and other complaints regarding the work environment,” the source said. “It was all part of a hostile culture we were trying to mitigate.”</p><p>Gatewood argues he created a “welcoming and open” work environment.</p><p>Mallory Harrity, a policy advisor to Gatewood, told the Sun-Times she never saw “any indication of hostility toward women.”</p><p>“This firing completely shocked me to the core,” Harrity said. “He’s [Gatewood] saving lives and making a difference.”</p><p>Another source familiar with the mayor’s office said there were concerns about Gatewood’s self-promotion, claiming he’d been booking his own appearances and applying to other jobs.</p><p>The sources said Gatewood was offered the opportunity to resign but refused and was instead fired.</p><p>Regardless of the rationale, the firing couldn’t be more ill-timed with summer months just around the corner, a time historically marked by spikes in crime.</p><p>Last year, Johnson largely credited “The People’s Plan for Community Safety,” a violence prevention initiative led by Gatewood, for the summer’s decades-low number of homicides.</p><p>Just this week Johnson’s office sent out a press release applauding a recent media report on the initiative that found crime was down by double digits in neighborhoods it targeted. </p><p>“I’m remarkably proud of the work that we were able to do,” Gatewood said. “This didn’t have anything to do with work. Just yesterday, they were tagging me on social media highlighting our work together. “</p><p>Johnson created the position of deputy mayor for community safety just hours after his inauguration in May 2023. He saw the role as crucial to delivering on a key campaign promise to confront the “root causes” of violent crime.</p><p>Gatewood, then director of the Illinois Justice Project, was his pick for the job.</p><p>At the Justice Project, Gatewood was charged with developing strategies to support young people, adults and returning citizens. He also provided behind-the-scenes support for bail reform in Illinois.</p><p>The prior iteration of the job, deputy mayor for public safety, became a revolving door under former Mayor Lori Lightfoot. Four different people took on that role in just three years.</p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: CPS revokes Aspira’s ability to operate charter schools in Chicago in rare move
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Board of Education voted unanimously Thursday to end its contract with the financially troubled Aspira charter network — the only time the school district has revoked a charter in the middle of the school year.</p><p>The charter will officially become inactive on April 4 and students will not be able to enroll at an Aspira school for next year.</p><p>Aspira’s two high schools started the school year with about 600 students, but the campuses are being emptied out. <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/03/05/aspira-will-soon-have-no-teachers-or-money-cps-is-still-struggling-to-close-it" target="_blank" ><u>Aspira’s leaders said the organization didn’t have enough money</u></a> to continue operating through the end of the school year, while the district said state law forbade it from <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/11/25/cps-to-help-aspira-make-payroll-but-charter-operators-financial-woes-remain" target="_blank" ><u>continuing to float Aspira as much cash</u></a> as it needed.</p><p>Board members did not comment before they canceled Aspira’s contract, but in the past, several said <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/11/19/chicago-public-schools-aspira-charter-network-finances-cps-investigation" target="_blank" ><u>Aspira’s poor financial management</u></a> necessitated the closure. They also said they felt terrible about the students having to transition midyear.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/03/19/former-aspira-students-try-to-adapt-after-troubled-charter-operator-closes-schools-midyear" >Former Aspira students try to adapt after troubled charter operator closes schools midyear</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The last time the school board took the dramatic action of revoking a charter contract <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2019/12/11/chicago-board-of-education-votes-to-close-two-charter-schools" target="_blank" ><u>was in December 2019</u></a>, though there have been other instances in which the school board did not renew a charter contract, or a charter operator decided to walk away. Over the past 10 years, more than 20 privately run charter or contract schools have closed in Chicago.</p><p>CPS interim CEO/Supt. Macquline King said she and her staff did not recommend Aspira’s charter revocation lightly.</p><p>“It follows a period of intensive monitoring of Aspira’s financial and operational stability, which CPS has determined to be severely lacking,” she told the board before the vote. “Despite our district providing $2.55 million in emergency financial advances to help maintain school operations, Aspira leadership has failed to provide a verified plan to fund operations through the remainder of the school year.”</p><p>Jen Conant, who works with unionized teachers at the Chicago Teachers Union, said at a news conference ahead of the board meeting that she hoped “today’s vote sends a clear signal that charter operators cannot play fast and loose with their money or with students' lives” and that “it helps us keep students from ever facing a crisis like this again.”</p><p>Aspira CEO Edgar Lopez told WBEZ on Wednesday that he was already talking to attorneys about next steps if the board went ahead with the revocation. He thinks he has a case because, he said, the school district provides charter schools less funding than they are supposed to receive under state law.</p><p>He was angry and distraught over the situation. He said he believes the vote to revoke Aspira’s charter was taken on behalf of the Chicago Teachers Union, which he argues controls the school board. The CTU represents Aspira’s school staff but has long been critical of publicly funded, privately run schools.</p><p>Lopez and other charter leaders have accused CPS of underfunding charter schools, and Lopez believes the loss of Aspira’s charter is retaliation for his speaking out about the issue.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-610000" name="image-610000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a08aeb3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5831x3273+0+312/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F1b%2F035456614e34af1346dc69e80525%2Faspiraclosing-260227-30.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d5f1c81/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5831x3273+0+312/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F1b%2F035456614e34af1346dc69e80525%2Faspiraclosing-260227-30.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/db9e626/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5831x3273+0+312/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F1b%2F035456614e34af1346dc69e80525%2Faspiraclosing-260227-30.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Aspira of Illinois President Edgar Lopez addressing school board members" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/db9e626/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5831x3273+0+312/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F1b%2F035456614e34af1346dc69e80525%2Faspiraclosing-260227-30.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9753a6e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5831x3273+0+312/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F1b%2F035456614e34af1346dc69e80525%2Faspiraclosing-260227-30.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/db9e626/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5831x3273+0+312/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2F1b%2F035456614e34af1346dc69e80525%2Faspiraclosing-260227-30.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Aspira of Illinois CEO Edgar Lopez addresses school board members at the Feb. 26 monthly meeting in an attempt to save the charter school network. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Talia Sprague/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>A legal battle might also ensue over Aspira’s school buildings. One of the two schools is in a modern, four-story building in Avondale. The other is in an older building that was renovated when Aspira put a school there.</p><p>Charter contracts say “any property or assets of the Charter School purchased with public funds for the operations of the Charter School … shall be returned to the Board, at no cost to the Board.”</p><p>When the school district <a class="Link" href="https://www.cpsboe.org/content/documents/march_19_2026_public_agenda_to_post.pdf" target="_blank" >laid out its rationale</a> for revoking the charter, officials noted that on Jan. 28, they issued an inquiry to Aspira about property transfers and “received information that ASPIRA transferred two of its properties to a newly created business entity” called the Aspira Illinois Foundation.</p><p>CPS asked Aspira for more details, but it is unclear if the district ever received the financial records it was seeking. CPS officials said in a statement that “accounting for all property and assets during wind-down is critical to protecting the Board’s interests and rights under the agreement.”</p><p>Lopez told WBEZ that the two properties transferred did not include Aspira’s schools. But he also said that he believes Aspira can retain those buildings as long as they use them for programs.</p><p>“This is not going to be turned into a for-profit usage in the buildings. That's not our intent, and it’s never going to happen,” Lopez said. “We're working on different plans, but CPS, they just want to kill us.”</p><p>Aspira of Illinois, the umbrella organization that includes the charter network, <a class="Link" href="https://aspirail.org/who-we-are/" target="_blank" >has been in operation for nearly 60 years</a>, and has some youth programming separate from the school. Aspira also runs an alternative school through a CPS contract with the organization Youth Connection Charter School. CPS says YCCS plans to keep the alternative school open at least through the end of the school year.</p><p>CPS and Aspira blame each other for the charter network’s downfall. CPS says that Aspira created budgets based on unrealistic enrollment projections, while Lopez says CPS’ student assignment system directed students away from charter schools like his. He said underfunding and low enrollment made the schools difficult to operate.</p><p>Conant of the CTU said the charter network’s mismanagement has <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/02/26/cps-to-shut-down-financially-distressed-aspira-campuses-scattering-students-to-new-schools" target="_blank" ><u>produced “chaos” for students and families</u></a>.</p><p>“Given the irresponsible management we have seen this year, and the cost to students' education, the district has no choice but to revoke their charter,” Conant said.</p><p>Conant applauded the district for helping students find new schools. Fewer than 50 students remain at the two closing Aspira schools, she added, and the operator has sent the CTU letters indicating they will have no staff or students after April 3. She said the district should now focus on making sure teachers can also join their students.</p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: State Rep. Margaret Croke wins Democratic primary for Illinois comptroller
entries.summary: <p>State Rep. Margaret Croke narrowly won the Democratic primary for Illinois comptroller Thursday, after almost two days of results hanging in the balance.</p><p>Croke <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/17/illinois-comptroller-democratic-primary-results" target="_blank" ><u>declared victory</u></a> late Tuesday, though her next-closest rival, state Sen. Karina Villa, did not concede as votes continued to trickle in. The two had been separated by just two percentage points. </p><p>By Thursday with 92.4% of expected votes counted, Croke had 34.6% of the vote, while Villa had 32.3%. The Associated Press called the race late Thursday afternoon.</p><p>“I am running for this office to be a fiscal watchdog who will protect taxpayer dollars, ensure transparency in how our state spends money, and pay bills on time,” Croke said in a statement Thursday. “I am incredibly grateful that my message is resonating with so many people across our state.”</p><p>Croke, 33, of Lincoln Park, also thanked her opponents, who also included Lake County Treasurer Holly Kim and state Rep. Stephanie Kifowit.</p><p>Kim, who outgoing Comptroller Susana Mendoza endorsed, received 24.4% of the vote, while Kifowit had 8.7%.</p><p>Villa’s campaign could not immediately be reached for comment. Late Tuesday, Villa said in a statement there were “still too many outstanding ballots in the mail and in drop boxes before a candidate is declared a winner.”</p><p>Villa, of West Chicago, won the plurality of votes in Chicago, and in DuPage and Kane counties. But Croke had advantages in suburban Cook County and several of the most populated downstate counties.</p><p>The race between Croke and Villa grew tighter in recent weeks as the two amassed the largest campaign funds in the race. </p><p>Backed by billionaire Gov. JB Pritzker and billionaire business executive Michael Sacks, Croke raised $1.8 million. Villa, largely backed by the Chicago Teachers Union, raised $1.4 million. </p><p>Croke also carried endorsements from Illinois House Speaker Emanuel "Chris" Welch and more than 30 other Democratic state lawmakers. Villa was endorsed by more progressive organizations and leaders such as U.S. Sen. Bernie Sanders, an independent from Vermont.</p><p>Croke will face Republican Bryan Drew in the general election in November. Drew ran uncontested in the GOP primary Tuesday.</p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Demócratas de Illinois hicieron campaña en contra de Trump. ¿Ahora qué sigue?
entries.summary: <p>Se acabó. ¿Ahora qué sigue? Esa es la pregunta para los demócratas de Illinois que se presentaron como luchadores feroces contra el presidente Donald Trump.</p><p>Illinois es un territorio fuertemente demócrata, por lo que es probable que los nominados en las elecciones primarias del martes se consoliden este noviembre.</p><p>Los votantes del área de Chicago han sido bombardeados con anuncios en medios de comunicación y digitales durante semanas con mensajes anti Trump, para los cargos del Condado de Cook, el estado, el Congreso y el Senado. En términos simples, los anuncios positivos presentan a los candidatos como luchadores contra Trump e ICE, mientras que una avalancha de anuncios negativos pinta a sus rivales como operativos de MAGA.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-ed0000" name="module-ed0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Decenas de millones de dólares de grupos y redes políticas a favor de las criptomonedas y de la inteligencia artificial (AI), conectados al Comité de Asuntos Públicos Estadounidense Israelí (AIPAC), alimentaron la incesante ofensiva publicitaria en las primarias demócratas. Estos grupos de interés especial nunca revelaron sus verdaderas agendas y no estamos mejor como comunidad al ser sometidos a este engaño político.</p><p>Sin embargo, podemos considerar lo que esto podría significar para los demócratas de cara a las elecciones generales en noviembre.</p><p>La oposición a Trump motivó a los demócratas de Illinois</p><p>Illinois tiene una de las primarias más tempranas del país, siguiendo la primaria del 3 de marzo junto con Arkansas, Carolina del Norte y Texas. En Mississippi fue el 10 de marzo. Sin embargo, Illinois es el primer gran estado azul donde los demócratas prueban el mensaje anti Trump antes de esas elecciones.</p><p>Por eso estas primarias nunca prestaron mucha atención a cuestiones muy locales y únicas. En este sentido, las contiendas de Illinois se nacionalizaron porque, dada la grave situación en la que nos encontramos, la oposición a Trump <i>es</i> el asunto local.</p><p>Y miremos lo que nuestra comunidad ha atravesado desde que Trump recuperó la Casa Blanca el año pasado: operativos del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) y la Patrulla Fronteriza; arrestos de personas en la calle; aterrorizando comunidades y deportando personas sin el debido proceso adecuado.</p><p>Además, recortes en la financiación federal para todo tipo de programas para castigar a Illinois por ser un estado azul que valora la atención médica, los inmigrantes, la asistencia alimentaria, la diversidad y todo tipo de derechos. Por si fuera poco, aranceles que impactan a las empresas locales y a tu bolsillo. Y mucho, mucho más.</p><p>No es de extrañar que los votantes nos dijeran el martes que <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/live/election-day-illinois-primaries-2026-results-analysis#0000019c-fcca-d6fc-a3fe-fddb0e980000" target="_blank" >la inmigración fue una de las principales razones</a> para acudir a las urnas.</p><p>Un golge de la realidad: los demócratas tienen poco poder</p><p>A los nuevos nominados: Den los detalles, por favor, sobre cómo ganar esta batalla cuesta arriba para salvar nuestra democracia. Es decir, sugieran algo que los demócratas no están haciendo ya. Porque todo lo que he escuchado en anuncios son gritos y teatralidades sobre luchar contra Trump.</p><p>Ya hay demócratas en los gobiernos locales, estatales y federales que ya están combatiendo a Trump. La realidad es que, si estos demócratas experimentados que ya están en el cargo pudieran detener a Trump, lo habrían hecho.</p><p>El gobernador JB Pritzker, renominado y listo para ganar otro mandato, dijo el martes por la noche que ha estado luchando contra Trump durante años. "Amigos, esta es la lucha de nuestras vidas”, dijo. "Todo lo que valoramos está bajo asedio por parte de Washington”.</p><p>No es por falta de voluntad. Cada demócrata se opone a Trump y quiere luchar. A menos que los demócratas logren cambiar el control de la Cámara o del Senado este noviembre, con poco poder, es difícil encontrar el camino.</p><p>En cuanto a la próxima lucha del pueblo contra Trump, hay programada una demostración "Sin Reyes" en el Butler Field de Grant Park para el 28 de marzo.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Lynn Sweet
